narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 37 (slightly above the 36.70 average), third-year school decile rank of 1 (much lower than the average 5.53), first-year decile of 3 (below the average 5.71), middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans—and predicted a 55.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability crossed the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your low third-year decile rank, which signals weaker performance relative to peers in your school and strongly raised concerns about your bar exam readiness. Next in importance, your LSAT score worked in your favor, as it was solid enough to pull the prediction somewhat toward passing, showing your potential in standardized testing. Your undergraduate GPA also helped a bit by nudging the odds slightly toward success, though it was still below average and couldn't overcome the bigger red flags. Compared to typical students who pass, your third-year grades stand out as particularly low, while your LSAT gives you an edge over many applicants. This combination meant the model saw higher risk in your profile overall. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve your decile rankings. Building a stronger narrative around your middle-income background and full-time commitment could also help in reapplying or appealing to other schools. With targeted improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor and pursue your legal goals.
